Once it has been calculated and saved, the drill-downs can then directly be visualized in the “Analytics” tab of the Analyze ESG Data application

Upcoming Features & Further Resources

  • Key priorities for upcoming releases include displaying targets within the “Analytics” tab, enabling multi-selection in the global filter for reporting structures, support for more chart types with saved custom chart views and saving “Analytics” settings for the whole page across multiple charts further enhancing flexibility and usability. 
  • If you are driving a project with SCT, please upvote or submit improvement requests via SAP Influence to ensure that future updates align with real-world needs and use cases. 
  • For further details, consult the SAP Help Portal and the SAP Roadmap for SCT:
    • Using the Analyze ESG Data Application | SAP Help Portal
    • SAP SCT Roadmap
    • Don’t forget to subscribe the What’s New section within SAP Help:
      What's New in SAP Sustainability Control Tower | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Master Data Readiness: The Foundation for S/4HANA Transformation and AI Success
entry.content:
Why Data Readiness is No Longer Optional

In the rush to modernise ERP landscapes and adopt AI-driven insights, organisations often overlook a fundamental truth: the success of your S/4HANA transformation and AI initiatives is directly proportional to the quality of your master data. Poor data quality doesn't just slow down migration projects, it can derail AI use cases before they even begin and undermine the business value of your entire digital transformation investment.

As SAP Architects, we've seen the pattern repeatedly: organisations invest millions in S/4HANA implementations and AI platforms, only to discover that inconsistent customer records, duplicate vendor entries, and incomplete material master data render their advanced analytics and AI models ineffective. The promise of intelligent automation and predictive insights remains just that a promise when built on a foundation of fragmented, unreliable data.

  • 85% of AI projects fail to deliver promised value (Gartner)
  • $3.1 trillion annually lost due to poor data quality (IBM)
  • Clear link between data quality and AI failure
  • 40% of business initiatives fail due to data quality issues (MIT Sloan Management Review)

The stakes have never been higher. As organisations race to leverage AI for competitive advantage, master data readiness has shifted from a technical necessity to a strategic imperative.

The Master Data Challenge in S/4HANA Transformations

S/4HANA transformations expose master data issues that may have been hidden or tolerated in legacy ECC environments. The simplified data model, real-time processing capabilities, and integrated analytics of S/4HANA demand a level of data quality and consistency that many organisations simply don't have.

Common Master Data Issues We Encounter:

Data Quality Problems:

  • Inconsistent naming conventions across entities (customers, materials, vendors)
  • Incomplete or missing critical attributes
  • Duplicate records with no clear system of record
  • Inaccurate hierarchies and organisational structures
  • Outdated or obsolete data cluttering the system

Data Governance Gaps:

  • Unclear ownership and accountability for data domains
  • Lack of standardised processes for data creation and maintenance
  • No enforcement of data quality rules
  • Multiple systems maintaining conflicting versions of truth
  • Absence of data stewardship roles and responsibilities

Integration Complexity:

  • Master data scattered across SAP and non-SAP systems
  • No unified view of critical business entities
  • Manual reconciliation processes prone to errors
  • Point-to-point integrations creating data silos
  • Legacy systems unable to provide clean data feeds

These challenges don't disappear when you move to S/4HANA they multiply. And when you layer AI initiatives on top of poor-quality data, you're essentially teaching your algorithms to learn from and perpetuate your data problems.

Why Master Data Quality is the Foundation of AI Success

Here's the reality that many organisations discover too late: AI is only as intelligent as the data it learns from. Machine learning models trained on incomplete, inconsistent, or biased data will produce unreliable predictions. Natural language processing applied to unstructured data with poor quality metadata will fail to deliver meaningful insights.

The Cost of Poor Data in AI:

  • AI models that produce unreliable predictions, eroding business trust
  • Bias amplification when training data contains historical data quality issues
  • Inability to achieve expected ROI on AI investments
  • Manual intervention required to correct AI outputs, defeating automation goals
  • Delayed or abandoned AI initiatives due to data readiness gaps
  • Compliance and ethical risks from biased or incomplete training data

The harsh truth: you cannot AI-transform your way out of a data quality problem. Clean, governed, and unified master data is not a nice-to-have it's the prerequisite for AI success.

 

The Hidden Complexity: Unifying SAP and Non-SAP Data

One of the most underestimated challenges in achieving data readiness is the distributed nature of enterprise data. Critical master data doesn't live in SAP alone it's scattered across:

Common Non-SAP Master Data Sources:

CRM Systems, PLM Platforms, E-commerce Platforms, MDM Solutions, Procurement Systems and homegrown Applications to name a few.

 

The Integration Challenge

For S/4HANA transformations, this distributed landscape creates significant complexity:

  • Multiple versions of truth for the same business entity
  • No single system owns the golden record
  • Manual reconciliation required before migration
  • Risk of migrating conflicting or duplicate data
  • Difficulty tracing data lineage and transformation logic

For AI initiatives, the challenge is even more acute:

  • AI models require comprehensive data across all systems
  • Training data must represent the complete business context
  • Incomplete data leads to biased or unreliable models
  • Real-time AI use cases need synchronised data across systems
  • Data scientists spend most of their time on data preparation instead of model development
  •  

Building the Foundation: A Unified Approach to Master Data

The solution requires a unified master data strategy that brings together SAP and non-SAP data into a single, governed, and trusted source of truth. This foundation serves both your S/4HANA transformation and your AI journey.

Key Components of a Unified Master Data Strategy:

  1. 1. Centralised Master Data Management

Establish a central hub for managing critical business entities:

  • Single authoritative source for customers, products, suppliers, and financial master data
  • Unified data model that spans SAP and non-SAP systems
  • Golden record creation through intelligent matching and merging
  • Real-time synchronisation to downstream systems
  • API-first architecture for AI and analytics consumption
  1. Data Quality and Governance Framework

Implement systematic data quality management:

  • Automated data quality rules and validation checks
  • Duplicate detection and resolution workflows
  • Data standardisation and enrichment processes
  • Workflow-driven governance with clear ownership
  • Continuous monitoring and quality KPIs
  • Data stewardship programs with accountability

Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

Pitfall 1: Underestimating Data Cleansing Effort

The Problem: Organisations allocate less of project time to data cleansing when it realistically requires lots more.

Pitfall 2: Lack of Business Ownership

The Problem: Treating master data as an IT problem rather than a business accountability.

Pitfall 3: Ignoring Non-SAP Systems

The Problem: Focusing solely on SAP data while critical master data resides in other solutions.

Pitfall 4: "We'll Clean It Up After Go-Live"

The Problem: Deferring data quality issues to post-implementation, where they're exponentially harder and more expensive to address.

Pitfall 5: Treating Master Data and AI as Separate Initiatives

The Problem: Running master data improvement and AI development as independent projects with different teams and timelines.

Pitfall 6: Building Point Solutions Instead of Platforms

The Problem: Creating custom data quality tools and integration scripts that become technical debt and can't scale to AI demands.

 

The Business Case: Why Data Readiness Pays

While master data initiatives require investment, the ROI is compelling when you consider both S/4HANA and AI value realisation:

Quantifiable Benefits:

  • 30-40% reduction in S/4HANA migration timeline and risk
  • 50-70% improvement in AI model accuracy and reliability
  • 25-35% reduction in manual data reconciliation efforts
  • 60% faster time-to-deployment for new AI use cases
  • Elimination of duplicate payments and customer records
  • 20-30% reduction in data-related support incidents

Strategic Benefits:

  • Foundation for data-driven and AI-powered decision making
  • Enablement of real-time analytics and predictive insights
  • Improved customer experience through unified views and personalisation
  • Regulatory compliance and audit readiness for AI governance
  • Competitive advantage through faster, more reliable AI innovation
  • Reduced risk of AI bias and ethical issues from poor data

AI-Specific Value:

  • Data scientists spend less time on data preparation, more on model development
  • Higher model accuracy translates to better business outcomes
  • Faster experimentation and iteration on AI use cases
  • Reduced risk of AI project failure due to data quality issues
  • Ability to scale AI across the enterprise with confidence

The question isn't whether you can afford to invest in master data readiness it's whether you can afford not to, given the risks to your S/4HANA transformation and the potential lost value from underperforming AI initiatives.

 

Summary: The Path Forward

Master data readiness is not a one-time project it's a continuous discipline that requires the right strategy, platforms, processes, and people. As S/4HANA transformations and AI initiatives converge, the quality of your master data becomes the single most important factor in determining success or failure.

Key Takeaways:

  1. Data Quality is the AI Foundation - Poor master data quality will undermine AI initiatives regardless of how sophisticated your models are. Garbage in, garbage out applies more than ever.
  2. Unify SAP and Non-SAP Data - AI requires comprehensive data across your entire enterprise. Build a unified master data strategy that spans all systems, not just SAP.
  3. Start Early, Start Strategic - Data readiness must begin before S/4HANA migration and AI deployment, not after. Build the foundation correctly from day one.
  4. Governance is Non-Negotiable - Technology alone won't solve data quality problems. Establish clear ownership, stewardship, and accountability for data quality.
  5. Think Platform, Not Point Solutions - Invest in enterprise master data management platforms that can scale with your S/4HANA and AI ambitions. Solutions like SAP Business Data Cloud can help accelerate your AI journey by providing the unified, governed data foundation AI demands.
  6. Measure What Matters - Establish data quality KPIs and track AI model performance improvements attributable to better data. Make the business case visible and ongoing.
  7. Integration is Key - Build robust integration architecture that can handle real-time AI demands while maintaining data quality and governance across SAP and non-SAP systems.

 

Conclusion

The convergence of S/4HANA transformations and AI adoption creates both unprecedented opportunity and a critical dependency: the quality of your master data. Organisations that recognise this dependency and invest in comprehensive master data readiness will accelerate their transformations, de-risk their migrations, and unlock the full potential of AI-driven insights and automation.

Those that continue to treat master data as an afterthought will find themselves trapped in a cycle of rework, delayed value realisation, and unreliable AI outputs regardless of how much they invest in cutting-edge technology.

The foundation of digital transformation and AI success isn't S/4HANA or machine learning platforms it's the quality, consistency, and accessibility of your master data across your entire enterprise landscape. Whether you leverage SAP Business Data Cloud, another master data management solution, or build your own platform, the imperative remains the same: unified, governed, AI-ready master data is non-negotiable.

For AI initiatives specifically, the data readiness challenge is even more acute. AI models are only as good as the data they learn from. Clean, comprehensive, and well-governed master data across SAP and non-SAP systems isn't just nice to have it's the difference between AI that transforms your business and AI that disappoints.

The question for SAP Architects is simple: are you ready to make master data readiness the strategic priority it needs to be for both your S/4HANA transformation and your AI journey?

The organisations that answer "yes" and act decisively will be the ones leading their industries with AI-powered innovation built on a foundation of trusted data. The others will be left wondering why their expensive AI investments failed to deliver and the answer will be staring them in the face: they skipped the foundation.

 

The window of competitive advantage is closing. Organisations that built their data foundation correctly are already deploying AI at scale improving customer experiences, optimising operations, and making better decisions faster. Those still struggling with basic data quality aren't just behind on AI adoption they're watching the gap widen every quarter.

AI doesn't wait for you to fix your data. Your competitors who prioritised data quality are already leveraging AI to eat your market share.

The choice is stark: invest in master data readiness now and unlock AI's potential or continue treating data quality as "someone else's problem" and watch your AI initiatives fail one by one.

SAP is Here to Help

Recognising that master data readiness is a critical challenge for organisations embarking on S/4HANA and AI journeys, SAP offers dedicated services designed to guide you through this transformation:

SAP Data Readiness Discovery Workshop

SAP Business Data Cloud Discovery Workshop

These workshops provide the expertise, frameworks, and actionable insights to accelerate your master data journey. SAP's experienced Architects work alongside your teams to assess your unique challenges, design practical solutions, and establish the data foundation that both S/4HANA and AI success demand.

You don't have to navigate the complexity of master data readiness alone. SAP is here to help and advice customers on building the data foundation that unlocks the full potential of digital transformation and AI innovation.

Ready to begin your master data readiness journey? Reach out to your SAP account team to learn more about these discovery workshops and how SAP can support your organisation's path to data excellence.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in February
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in February

February depression—no way!
Plenty of great features and enhancements being delivered with our two releases in SAP Datasphere and BDC, and SAP Analytics Cloud also delivered a packed quarterly release.

Learn more in my short top features video in February 2026 on YouTube 📺 as well as the QRC1 2026 release for SAP Analytics Cloud by @Orla_C .

In addition, explore the latest updates in our community news blogs and more.

 

My top features in February

 

Data Pipeline Analyzer

The Data Pipeline Analyzer is a monitoring tool designed for real-time analysis of complex data models, providing immediate visibility into errors, warnings, and data discrepancies. By focusing on node status indicators and streamlined navigation, the tool allows users to pinpoint the exact location of data transfer failures. This targeted troubleshooting approach ensures efficient oversight of flows, enabling organizations to maintain high data quality and resolve issues promptly to prevent downstream disruptions.

➡️  More information.

 

Task Chain Output Parameter

After introducing input parameters already in Q3 last year, you can now use output parameters in task chains. You can also map output parameters to the task chain from the task object. This will allow for more flexible usage of task chains and nested task chains.

➡️  More information.

 

Transformation Flow Object Versioning

The versioning concept you know for other modeling objects is now available for transformation flows. You can now review and restore versions, and the general options are similar to other editors where versioning is already available. You can:

  • Open a past version in read-only mode.
  • Download a past version to a CSN/JSON file.
  • Restore a past version to replace the current version.

➡️  More information.

 

Local Table (Files) Delete Inbound Buffer

Local Tables (File) store a large amount of data, including delta capture changes and historical data. When data is updated, the information is kept on your local table (file) with the delta changes columns, and a new version is stored in the object store. Keeping all these records and versions can consume a lot of memory, and can impact performance.
You can now delete records waiting in the inbound buffer of a local table (file), even if a merge task has not run.

➡️  More information.

 

Visibility of Data Access Controls Applied to Sources

A small but helpful feature, where you can now see data access controls applied to the sources of your view in a subsection Applied via Sources under the list of Data Access Controls in the side panel.

➡️  More information.

 

Sharing of Analytic Models

We all waited a long time for the cross-space sharing of an analytic model together with its contributing entities like the fact, dimensions, hierarchies, and text entities. You can share an analytic model with one or even with multiple spaces.

This enables users in the target space with the ability to stack on the model, inheriting its properties and enhancing the new model to meet their needs.

➡️  More information.

 

 

SAP Analytics Cloud Feature Highlights QRC1

The first release for SAP Analytics Cloud for 2026 is already out and @orla Cullen dives a little deeper into the top 5 features of the first quarter:

🔹New Chart Types – Expanded native visualizations with Area and Pareto charts to simplify advanced analysis.

🔹Story Versioning – Create, manage, and restore up to 10 major story versions for better iterative control.

🔹Comment Management – A new dashboard to search, filter, copy, and bulk-delete comments across models and versions.

🔹My Metrics Alerting – Subscribe to metric reports, schedule delivery, and get in-app/email notifications when metrics change.

🔹Live Connectivity to Snowflake – Extended external live data access for planning models connected directly to Snowflake.

 

 

 

Webinar Series: The Next Era of Business Data

SAP Business Data Cloud is part of a comprehensive strategy for enterprise data designed to address complex enterprise data management challenges. We provide an overview of SAP Business Data Cloud and guide you through its integral pillars and strategic vision.

👉 Sign up now: SAP BDC: The Next Era of Business Data

🔹Jan 29 | Discover SAP Business Data Cloud in 2026 - recording

🔹Feb 05 | The Smart Path to SAP BW Modernization - recording

🔹Feb 19 | Empowering SAP Datasphere Users with SAP Business Data Cloud Innovations - recording

🔹Feb 26 | From Data to Decisions: Analytics with SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP BDC

🔹Mar 12 | From Data Silos to Business Insight: The Power of BDC Connect

🔹Mar 19 | An Architect's Blueprint for SAP Business Data Cloud as the Foundation for Enterprise AI

entry.title: BI Remediation Checks during ECC to S/4HANA Migration
entry.content:
As SAP prepares to end support for ECC by 2027, many organizations are making the crucial move to SAP S/4HANA. The shift can unlock improved capabilities and performance, but it can also introduce changes that ripple through the BI landscape if we don’t plan for them early.

From a BI perspective, there are multiple aspects to consider during the transition. This blog focuses on the BI remediation checks that help protect data trust, reporting, and decision-making continuity during and after the migration.

Why do we need BI remediation? 
The migration from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA introduces significant changes to the underlying data structures. These changes impact how data is extracted, modelled, and consumed in the BI system. Because of that, reporting remediation becomes critical to ensure data consistency, accuracy, and continuity in analytics after the migration.

With remediation, we ensure:

  • BI models and reports remain functional and aligned with the new S/4HANA structures
  • Disruption is minimized for business operations and reporting processes

Data model simplifications in S/4HANA (what changes underneath)
When SAP S/4HANA was first introduced, the SAP ERP 6.0 data model underwent major restructuring. Redundancies were removed, and many smaller tables were consolidated into fewer, larger ones.
Figure 1 can help visualize the concept and why “what worked in ECC” may not behave the same way in S/4HANA.

                                                                                                 FIGURE 1

Impact of S/4HANA migration on the BI system (what to check)

1) Impact on BW Datasources / Extractors
If you run SAP BW, one of the first questions is:
“After ECC becomes S/4HANA, will my extractors and DataSources still be supported and behave the same way?”
SAP note 2500202  provides an Excel sheet produced by SAP that details the impact of S/4HANA on BW Datasources. This sheet is periodically updated. The same note also contains a PPT that guides us on how to interpret and use the Excel list.

A proven approach is:

  • Inventory your active extractors (e.g., via ROOSOURCE).  
  • Identify which are truly used (process chains / BI statistics where available)
  • Cross-check them against the SAP note 2500202 list and categorize outcomes (no change / restricted / obsolete / alternative required)

2) Tables with Replacement Objects (don’t rely on “classic tables” blindly)
If, as part of your BI remediation strategy, you plan to continue using certain classic ECC tables, for example, relying on MARD instead of adopting the newer MATDOC structure, you must depend on the corresponding Replacement Object rather than accessing the table directly.
With the data model simplification in SAP S/4HANA, many traditional ECC tables are no longer the authoritative “single source of truth.” In many cases, data is aggregated or derived via CDS views that join and enrich information from multiple sources. Because of this shift, it becomes essential to transition from reading classic tables (such as MARC, MARD, and others) to using their designated Replacement Objects, ensuring that analytics and integrations receive accurate, up‑to‑date data.

  • In Native HANA, it is advisable to verify whether a table has a replacement object or whether it can still be used directly.
  • In SAP BW, the datasource typically absorbs most of the underlying data model changes. However, in hybrid scenarios, particularly when calculation views are built on ECC base tables or when custom datasources built on ECC base tables, it becomes essential to thoroughly assess the impact on the affected tables.

Figure 2 shows an example for table MARD. NSDM_E_MARD serves as the replacement view for the classic MARD table. Although data continues to reside in MARD, those entries generally represent older documents migrated to S/4HANA or new S/4HANA records that are stored without measure fields. To obtain the full set of information (both characteristics and measures) for transactions created in S/4HANA, you must use the replacement object, in this case NSDM_E_MARD, rather than relying on MARD directly.

                                                                                                     FIGURE 2

3) Replacement of obsolete tables
Some ECC tables are replaced or made obsolete, and direct reporting on such tables is no longer recommended or supported. Examples tables include:

  • KONV, VBUP, VBUK, EIKP, EIPO

Obsolete tables may still exist, but they can behave as compatibility views. For accuracy and performance, they should be switched to transparent tables where applicable.

  • SAP Note 3250204: describes about the replacement table PRDC_ELEMENTS for KNOV table.
  • SAP Note 2198647: talks about tables VBUP, VBUK and SD data model changes.
  • SAP Note 3482527: covers about new S/4HANA foreign trade. 

4) Key fields changed (primary key shifts can break pipelines)
In S/4HANA, the primary keys of some tables have changed compared to ECC. Some examples are:

  • VBFA
  • FAAV_ANLC

These changes directly affect how data is stored, identified, and retrieved. That can break:

  • Joins in HANA models
  • Deduplication logic
  • Delta logic or key-based transformations in BW

Action

  • Review where these tables are used in extraction/transformation layers
  • Re-check join conditions, uniqueness assumptions, and surrogate key logic
  • Re-test deltas and aggregations end-to-end

5) Field length extensions (small change, big downstream effect)
Some fields were extended in S/4HANA to support broader functional requirements, enhanced integration needs, and future extensibility.
One such example is:

  • VBAP-VBTYP (Char1) Document Category replaced by VBAP-VBTYPL (Char4)
    • SAP Note 2495386 details the BW datasource that uses this object.

Another major example:

  • Material Number (SAP Note 2215424) changed from char(18) to char(40)

A key design decision regarding material numbers is whether your organization will:

  • Adopt a true 40‑character material number, introducing a new business meaning, or
  • Retain the existing 18‑character logic, while simply expanding the technical field length to 40 characters.

The chosen approach directly influences how material numbers flow through your BI data models and determines the adjustments required in downstream reporting and integrations.

6) Centralized Master Data via Business Partner (Customer/Vendor integration)
SAP fundamentally changed how master data is managed by introducing a unified, centralized model for customers, vendors, and business partners. This replaces the traditional ECC siloed structures with a harmonized object: the Business Partner (BP).
It’s not a “technical-only” change, it’s a semantic change in how master data is governed
Please check the SAP Note 2265093 note for more information.

7) Redirecting ECC Virtual Tables in HANA Calculation Views
When your BI models use calculation views that reference ECC base tables, these views must be updated after the migration to ensure they point to the corresponding objects in the S/4HANA system. Redirecting them is essential to prevent calculation view failures and to avoid inconsistent or incorrect analytical results after the migration. Please refer the blog for mitigation of this impact.

 
Final thoughts: ECC to S/4HANA migration is not only an ERP transformation, but also a BI continuity challenge. The safest approach is to treat BI remediation as a structured workstream, validating extractors, replacing obsolete dependencies, and proactively addressing table/key/field changes before they surface as production reporting issues.

If this post helps you structure your BI remediation checks, please consider sharing your own experiences or additions in the comments.

entry.title: Introducing Advanced Commodity Risk Analytics (ACRA)
entry.content:
As part of our ongoing commitment to continuously improve SAP Commodity Management, we are excited to introduce Advanced Commodity Risk Analytics (ACRA),a next-generation product designed to meet the growing complexity and scale of modern commodity trading operations.

Commodity markets are evolving rapidly, with increasing data volumes, tighter reporting timelines, and greater demands for transparency and performance. ACRA is built to address these challenges head-on, providing a robust, future-ready foundation for commodity risk analytics.

What is ACRA?

Advanced Commodity Risk Analytics (ACRA) is a comprehensive risk analytics solution that reimagines how commodity risk is calculated, processed, and reported.

At the core of ACRA is a complete redesign of the existing Risk Analytics architecture. This redesign spans, over a series of releases, the entire analytics lifecycle—from commodity position management and mark-to-market (MtM) calculations to trading profit and loss (P&L) reporting. The result is a standardized, scalable risk analytics product capable of supporting the most demanding commodity trading environments.

Our objective is simple but ambitious: to deliver a high-performance, cloud-native Risk Analytics solution that can manage extensive data volumes while simplifying adoption and lowering the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for our customers.

Why ACRA? Key Benefits

ACRA is built with both today’s requirements and tomorrow’s needs in mind. Key benefits include:

Scalability

Analytical processes are decoupled from transactional processes. As a result, they are no longer constrained by the resources of the SAP S/4HANA transactional system. These analytical processes operate as a pure cloud application and can scale naturally.

High Performance

Risk calculation is divided into simplified stacks that can be processed simultaneously in parallel threads. Persisting calculation results delivers further enhancements to reporting performance.

Future-Proof Architecture

Using modern cloud architecture and an API-first open interface design, SAP, partners, and customers can easily introduce advanced risk analytics features and custom business logic.

Seamless Integration

Native integration exists between the transactional system SAP S/4HANA and the risk analytics platform. This eliminates the need for a separate custom interface or any side-car solutions.

Delivery Flexibility

The release cycles of a cloud application are independent of the SAP S/4HANA release cycles. This speed and flexibility enable faster innovation cycles for our customers accelerating benefit realisation.

Core Features Available Today

ACRA delivers powerful functionality right out of the gate, enabling organizations to modernize their commodity risk operations immediately.

Risk Calculation Engine

  • Introduce a tailored, modular risk calculation engine for Position Reporting
  • Perform only the necessary risk calculation steps, which shortens processing time and makes the calculation workflow easier to maintain and extend
  • Enable running multiple calculation tasks at the same time to speed up processing
  • Introduce a refined integration data model for SAP S/4HANA, which reduces redundant data storage and improves consistency between transactional data and risk results
  • Executes risk calculations in the cloud (outside the SAP S/4HANA core system), which frees up core system’s resources and avoids running duplicate calculations
  • Store calculated results in the cloud as the system of record, using a two-tier storage strategy – a fast ‘hot’ layer for recent data and a lower-cost ‘cold’ layer for historical archives – to optimize performance and costs

Snapshot and Scheduling Cockpit

  • Schedule regular activities such as market data quality checks, Position calculations, end-of-day and end-of-month result validation and day-over-day comparison.
  • Provide automation of routine tasks and a clear sequence and status of the workflow – Risk analysts receive notifications when any calculation step errors out and can access error logs for diagnosis to improve control by allowing key users (e.g. risk managers) to review and approve results in a dedicated workflow before the figures are published to all users and management.

Manage Market Curves

Market Curves are centrally managed and linked to:

  • Market Products
  • Market Locations
  • Risk Domains (such as profit centres or global regions assigned to company codes)

Each Market Curve contains relevant price indexes—such as Derivative Contracts and Basis IDs—ensuring that related risk is accurately calculated and reported across the organization.

ACRA supports complex market curve definitions – you can combine multiple price indices with custom weightings or adjustments (like basis differentials) as long as their date ranges don’t overlap, instead of having to create separate curves for each scenario.

Manage Maturity Calendars

Maturity Calendars are assigned to the Derivative Contract Specification (DCS) and enable:

  • Definition of the market norm/orthodoxy
  • Early market rolls
  • Default period derivation directly from SAP S/4HANA DCS settings

This ensures consistency between trading configurations and risk analytics while providing flexibility where required.

End-of-Day (EoD) Processing

ACRA integrates with SAP’s Advanced Financial Closing (AFC) to run a robust end-of-day process that prepares consolidated position reports

This provides flexibility through:

  • Sequencing pre-delivered risk calculation steps for commodity risk calculations (Derivatives and Logistics)
  • custom processing steps to address company-specific requirements (e.g., proprietary calculations or data validation checks)
  • Include manual verification steps (for example, requiring a manager’s sign-off) to ensure accuracy before proceeding.

The result is a controlled, auditable, and flexible EoD process.

Consolidated Position End of Day Reporting

Using SAP Datasphere data models and analytical clients such as SAP Analytics Cloud, ACRA delivers comprehensive risk reporting, from high-level summaries down to individual commodity contracts and inventory positions.

Users gain full visibility into their End-of-Day positions with drill-down capabilities that support better decision-making and governance.

Conclusion - A Modern Foundation for Commodity Risk

ACRA represents a significant step forward in commodity risk analytics—combining performance, scalability, and a future-proof design into a single standardized solution. By modernizing the architecture and simplifying adoption, ACRA empowers commodity trading organizations to manage risk more effectively today while staying ready for what’s next.

Visit SAP Help Portal for more an Administration Guide: 

https://help.sap.com/docs/CCS/7b6b503b6ae0446fb3d6801c98f56b1e/349e9247507644e8a8df8a20cb60af8b.html?locale=en-US

Stay tuned for future innovations by following SAP Roadmap Explorer  for Commodity Management: 

https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?PRODUCT=42F2E964F4E71EDAA6A3587248CD40E5&range=CURRENT-LAST

entry.title: Why Attend The Fabric of Data & AI: Insights from Google, Ericsson, Vodafone & More
entry.content:
JOIN US AT THE FABRIC OF DATA & AI ON MARCH 24, 2026, 11AM EDT | 11AM CET | 1PM SGT

AI without context is like walking to the car wash.  

You may have seen the viral video: someone asks ChatGPT whether to walk or drive to the car wash. AI analyzes distance, time, and efficiency, then recommends walking. It misses the obvious: you need the car there to wash it. 

It's funny, until you realize the same disconnect is happening in business decisions every day. AI can analyze data, optimize metrics, and execute quickly, but without understanding business context, it can provide technically correct answers that completely miss the mark. 

As organizations deploy AI agents, intelligent applications, and large language models, many are experiencing a critical gap: the data strategies that powered the last decade of innovation weren't designed for autonomous decision-making. 

The Fabric of Data & AI, a virtual summit on March 24, 2026, brings together industry leaders at the forefront of this transformation. Join Irfan Khan, President & Chief Product Officer of SAP Data & Analytics, in conversation with leaders from Google, Ericsson, Vodafone, Team Liquid, Mead Johnson Nutrition, and the Open Data Institute. 

Here are three reasons you should attend. 

1. Understand why yesterday’s data strategies fall short 

For decades, data strategies have focused on extracting data into centralized systems for applications and dashboards. This approach served its purpose for traditional analytics, but it created a problem: the loss of important business context. 

When AI makes critical decisions, such as allocating inventory, prioritizing customers, and managing cash flow, it needs more than just accurate data. It needs to grasp the business relationships, priorities, and trade-offs that determine the right course of action. 

At this summit, you'll discover: 

  • What happens when business context gets lost as data moves across systems 
  • Why AI agents can optimize individually but create problems collectively 
  • How preserving business meaning by design creates a competitive advantage 

2. Learn from leaders solving the same challenges you face

You’ll hear directly from leaders who are building data and AI strategies in their organizations, including: 

  • Irfan Khan, President & Chief Product Officer, SAP Data & Analytics 
  • Jannie Affeld, VP Finance Systems and ERP, Google 
  • Malin Persson, CIO & Head of Enterprise IT, Ericsson 
  • Ricard Rovira Marti, Head of Corporate IT Platforms, Vodafone 
  • Steve Arhancet, Co-CEO and Co-Owner, Team Liquid 
  • Ganesh Sivakumar, Global Chief Data Analytics & AI Officer, Mead Johnson Nutrition 
  • Louise Burke, Chief Executive Officer, Open Data Institute 

Each brings different approaches, unique challenges, and proven outcomes from real-world experience across finance, telecommunications, technology, esports, and consumer goods. 

3. Gain actionable insights for your organization 

Develop a strategic perspective on the decisions that determine AI success. Join us for: 

  • Clarity on the shift: Discover why shared organizational knowledge is becoming a structural requirement for AI success. 
  • The architectural framework: Learn how a business data fabric connects data, processes, and business rules to keep AI grounded in business context. 
  • The integration model: See how technology, process, and culture must evolve together, and why all three are essential for success. 

Whether you lead data and analytics, drive business strategy, or oversee technology implementation, this event provides the insights to successfully navigate this transition. 

Save your spot 

The conversation about data and AI is everywhere, but most of it focuses on what AI can do, not what it needs to succeed. Don't miss this opportunity to learn from the leaders who are building the foundation for reliable AI. 

Register now for The Fabric of Data & AI and join the conversation on March 24, 2026.

entry.title: Welcome to the Data Professionals Community
entry.content:
Finding relevant, meaningful information about data and AI practices can feel overwhelming. Connecting with like-minded professionals who work with data every day remains even more challenging. The Data Professionals interest group bridges this gap. This community brings together practitioners at every stage of their journey in support of a common mission: to learn, connect, and discover new experiences that advance work with data and AI.

Your connection for collaboration and growth

The group welcomes perspectives across all data and AI topics. These types of projects deliver their greatest value when a team of colleagues work together towards the final solution. Thus, the spirit of this community.

Engage, contribute, and solve

The interest group invites active participation through many options:

  • Discussions for sharing implementation challenges, solutions, and lessons learned.
  • Blog posts for showcasing expertise and thought leadership.
  • Knowledge base articles for reading practical guidance on common scenarios.
  • Events for finding upcoming webinars and workshops that deepen skills.

Join and become part of our growing community. Follow topics that are meaningful to you. Connect with a broader network of like-minded individuals who are passionate about transforming data into business value. Your journey alongside a global group of Data Professionals can begin today.

Let’s get the conversation started! What data or AI challenge have you recently solved that could help someone else?

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud for the Business Analyst: turning raw data to trusted and actionable insight
entry.content:
If you’re a business analyst in a center of excellence or embedded in a line-of-business team, you know the moment. It’s late in the day, you’re juggling closing activities, and someone pings you: “Our KPI moved. What changed?” You open your dashboard, spot the variance, and start tracing the root cause, only to hit the usual constraints: the definition of the KPI is unclear, the numbers don’t match what Finance sees, and the clock is not on your side.

Many of us have been in that seat. And the hardest part isn’t building a nice visualization; it’s getting to a trusted and actionable insight fast enough that the business can do something with it. When definitions conflict across teams, when data is scattered across SAP and non-SAP systems, and when every new question turns into a new item in the request queue, time-to-decision becomes the issue.

That’s where SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) aims to help business analysts: it harmonizes SAP application data and external data while preserving business semantics, so analytics and AI can stay trustworthy and practical for day-to-day fact-based decisions.

Why “trusted” is what makes “fast” possible

We don’t lose time because we can’t analyze, we lose time because we can’t connect and align. One team’s “net revenue” becomes another team’s “net revenue after returns.” One pipeline forecast uses an updated hierarchy while another uses last week’s mapping. The outcome is predictable: rework, credibility damage, and the dreaded “Which number is right?” loop.

As a business analyst, you succeed when you turn business data into actionable insight, bridging business users and data experts through validation, visualization, and clear communication. The path to faster insight-to-action starts when the platform reduces ambiguity and makes consistent definitions easier to reuse.

BDC’s value for business analysts shows up through three practical use cases—agentic analytics, data storytelling, and composable analytics—all aimed at accelerating the route to trusted, actionable answers.

Agentic analytics: when the platform helps you spot the signal

Agentic analytics are AI-driven, context-aware analytics that continuously monitor changing data conditions and deliver relevant insights and recommendations with minimal human intervention. In real terms, it’s the difference between discovering the KPI variance in tomorrow’s meeting versus being alerted early enough to act today.

BDC emphasizes two enablers here: AI-powered intelligent applications that surface patterns and anomalies, and a context-rich data foundation that unifies SAP and third-party data into a harmonized semantic layer, so the insight is explainable and based on consistent meaning.

When you’re under pressure, closing, forecast updates, pipeline reviews, this matters because it reduces the time you spend hunting for “what changed” and increases the time you can spend on “what should we do next.”

Data storytelling: turn analysis into a decision, not a debate

Even when you have the right answer, stakeholders still need the “why” in plain language. Data storytelling augments dashboards and visualizations with narrative context so decision-makers understand implications and act.

BDC highlights automated narratives (natural language generation that turns charts into written explanations) and in-context collaboration (comments, annotations, and sharing directly on dashboards). This directly supports the reality of LoB-embedded analysis: you’re not just producing charts, you’re aligning people around what the KPI means, why it moved, and what action to take.

Composable analytics: keep insight in the flow of work

Composable analytics treats models, metrics, and visualization widgets as reusable building blocks that can be combined and embedded into applications or workflows. For a business analyst, this is about scale: fewer one-off rebuilds, more reusable KPI logic, and more ways to deliver insight where decisions are made.

BDC positions open integration and embedding plus a unified semantic layer and reusable metrics as the foundation. The practical win is consistency: when you reuse a KPI definition across dashboards (and across teams), the conversation shifts from “whose number is right?” to “what are we going to do about it?”

A practical, analyst-first workflow to cut time-to-insight

Here’s the workflow you wish you’d had earlier, optimized for faster time to trusted and actionable insight:

  1. Start with governed building blocks: anchor your dashboard and KPI logic on curated models or data products so definitions don’t drift week to week.
  2. Validate fast when pressure hits: when a variance appears during closing or forecast cycles, run targeted ad-hoc checks without abandoning governed definitions.
  3. Explain the “why,” not just the “what”: pair visuals with narrative so stakeholders understand the root cause and the next action.
  4. Reuse and embed what works: treat your KPIs, metrics, and story components as reusable assets so every pipeline review doesn’t require a rebuild.

That’s the shift from “I made a report” to “I accelerated a decision.”

Make your next business question easier than the last

If you’re a business analyst, you’re going to keep getting the same class of questions: KPI movement, pipeline change, forecast risk, closing variance, root cause. The difference is whether answering them feels like a scramble, or a repeatable workflow grounded in trusted definitions and faster insight loops.

To go further, browse the Business Analyst resources in this interest group and build familiarity with how SAP Business Data Cloud supports agentic analytics, data storytelling, and composable analytics in real analyst workflows.

If you want, share in the comments: What’s the business question you get most often during closing, forecasting, or pipeline reviews—and what usually slows you down: time, definitions, or conflicting numbers?

Resources

Blog Post: What is a Business Data Fabric?

Browse the latest analytics resources on SAP.com

entry.title: Administering Sustainability Control Tower
entry.content:
Why Administration Matters

Before organizations can effectively manage sustainability data, they need a solid technical foundation. SAP Sustainability Control Tower requires proper setup, role management, and system integration to ensure accurate, consistent, and scalable operations.

A well-administered system enables:

  • Reliable data integration across SAP landscapes
  • Secure and structured user access
  • Smooth operation for business users
  • A strong foundation for reporting and steering sustainability

What You’ll Learn in This Learning Journey

This compact, 1.5-hour course focuses on the essential administrative tasks required to get SAP Sustainability Control Tower up and running:

  1. Setting Up Accounts and Services
    Learn how to manage accounts and services to prepare the system for use.
  2. Assigning Roles
    Understand different user roles and how to assign them to ensure proper access control and governance.
  3. Configuring System Integration
    Connect SAP Sustainability Control Tower with key systems, including:
  • SAP S/4HANA
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • SAP Datasphere
  • SAP EHS Management
  • SAP Sustainability Footprint Management
  • SAP Analytics Cloud

This ensures seamless data exchange and a fully connected sustainability landscape.

Prerequisite: Basic knowledge from the Learning Journey Reporting and Steering Sustainability in Your Businesses using SAP Sustainability Control Tower.

Who Should Take the Course?

This learning journey is designed for:

  • System Administrators responsible for setup and configuration

Build the technical foundation for effective sustainability management and start the Learning Journey “Administering SAP Sustainability Control Tower” here.

entry.title: The Data Ownership dilemma — and how SAP Business Data Cloud Data Products helps resolve It
entry.content:
The core problem: Data belongs to the Business, but IT manages it

Many enterprises agree in principle that “data is a business asset.” The business generates the data, uses it daily, and relies on it for decision‑making. Yet, in practice, IT manages most of the systems, pipelines, and storage, creating a structural contradiction:

  • Business claims ownership, but
  • IT controls the handling.

This leads to a classic accountability gap:
Business expects IT to provide clean, reliable, well-modeled data, while IT expects business units to define rules, definitions, and quality expectations. The misunderstanding is structural.

When data is copied Into a Data Platform, the gap widens

As soon as data is replicated into a platform such as a data lake, data warehouse, the conversation changes:

  • The business often assumes that data copied into the platform becomes IT’s responsibility entirely.
  • IT, however, needs business input to validate transformations, definitions, mappings, and quality checks.

The outcome is predictable:

  1. IT publishes the replicated data, structuring it according to their best understanding.
  2. Business reviews it and complains:
    • “This data is not clean.”
    • “These definitions don’t match our business reality.”
    • “The numbers don’t reconcile with the source data.”

This friction exists because ownership is unclear at every stage: raw data, transformed data, curated views, and analytic models.

The hidden trap: Recreating an ERP inside a Data Platform

One of the most common pitfalls in this context is that all ERP intelligence ends -up being recreated in the data platform.
Why does this happen?
When business users review data in the data platform, they often compare results with ERP transactions. Any deviation is seen as an error. This pushes IT teams to:

  • Rebuild ERP logic,
  • Recreate document flows,
  • Mirror every table, relationship, and constraint,
  • Re‑implement ERP-specific business rules.

Recreating ERP logic leads to:

  • Massive data model complexity
  • Fragile pipelines that break when ERP updates occur
  • Endless reconciliation loops
  • Slow delivery cycles
  • High cost and low return

Data ownership must evolve — and the SAP SAP Business Data Cloud platform enable that evolution

The tension between business and IT will not disappear simply with new tooling. But SAP’s Business Data Cloud introduces capabilities that directly address the root causes:

Within SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP provides out-of-the-box, ready to consume predefined data flows and models based on every SAP applications : Data Products. These Data Products carry business semantics, and application context. They are perfectly aligned with the business definition of the data and ready to consume.

That means:

  • Business has access to the very same data than in their applications.
  • Data definitions are explicit.
  • IT handles the platform; business handles meaning.
  • Conflicts reduce because expectations are defined in the product lifecycle, supervised by SAP.

By providing curated, governed Data Products sourced directly from SAP applications, Data Products expose predefined structures and metadata, meaning you’re consuming business-ready, not system-ready, data.

This is how BDC helps addressing the data ownership problem :

Problem

Impact

How SAP BDC Helps

Business claims ownership but delegates responsibility to IT

Misunderstandings, poor quality feedback

Data Products enforce decentralized, domain-based ownership with structured metadata and lifecycle governance

Copying data creates ambiguity over who validates it

IT guesses business logic; business rejects results

Data Products include business semantics and expose data via standard interfaces that business can validate early

Recreating ERP logic in the data platform

Complex, costly, brittle architectures

BDC offers curated, SAP-native data products designed for analytics and AI without reconstructing operational logic

Conclusion: Data ownership is a shared responsibility — with the right tools

Data ownership cannot sit solely with IT or solely with the business. It must be shared and formalized.

SAP SAP Business Data Cloud , through its standardized Data Products, provides a practical way to bridge the gap:

  • Business defines semantics, meaning, and rules.
  • IT manages infrastructure, governance frameworks, and integration.
  • Both collaborate on iterative product lifecycles rather than one‑off pipeline deliveries.

Organizations adopting this model move from blame cycles to collaborative stewardship, unlocking faster delivery, higher trust, and cleaner data.

entry.title: ICYMI: The Fabric of Data & AI - Virtual Event
entry.content:
As agentic AI becomes mission-critical, organizations face an architectural gap - data strategies built for traditional transactional and analytic workloads must be revisited in order to supply AI agents with the right enterprise data. 

On March 24, SAP hosted a key virtual event highlighting the importance of a business data fabric in the architecture of successful AI projects.  The replay is now available. By registering, you can still learn how Google, Ericsson, and Vodafone ensure business context as AI scales.

You'll learn:

  • What AI agents need beyond data to make reliable decisions
  • How a business data fabric preserves meaning as AI scales
  • The first steps toward AI agents that deliver real business value

Register here: https://events.sap.com/fabric-of-data-and-ai/en_us/home.html

Also, check out this blog post to learn more about the speakers: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/the-fabric-of-data-amp-ai-meet-the-executive-speakers/ba-p/14352171

entry.title: SAP RPT-1 Meets Employee Attrition: A Hands-On Test with SuccessFactors Data
entry.content:
The Short Version

I wanted to see if SAP’s new tabular AI model, RPT-1, could predict which employees are likely to quit, using nothing but a CSV extracted from SuccessFactors fields. No model training. No data science team. Just upload and predict.

To keep things honest, I based the experiment on a real peer-reviewed study that used actual employee data and traditional ML. The result? RPT-1 nailed all 5 test predictions in under 400 milliseconds. But the full story is more interesting than the headline.

 

Why I Picked Attrition (and Not Another Sales Demo)

Credit where it's due: Hari Panakkal's recent SAP Community post, Experiment with SAP RPT-1: Predicting the Right Carrier in SAP TM Using the No-Code Playground, is a great walkthrough of RPT-1 on transportation data. It got me thinking: if RPT-1 works that well for carrier prediction in TM, what happens when you point it at an HR problem?

Most RPT-1 demos I've seen focus on sales forecasting or logistics. Those are solid use cases, but the one I care about is people analytics, and the single biggest question HR teams ask is: "Who's about to leave?"

Attrition is a natural fit for RPT-1 because:

  • The data is tabular and already sitting in SuccessFactors
  • The problem is binary classification (stays vs. leaves)
  • Most HR teams don't have ML engineers on staff

But I didn't want to just throw random HR data at it. I wanted to test RPT-1 against something with a known answer.

 

The Research Behind the Experiment

I grounded everything in this study:

Manafi Varkiani, S., Pattarin, F., Fabbri, T., & Fantoni, G. (2025). Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants. Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.

Here’s what makes this paper worth paying attention to:

It used real data. Not overused Kaggle dataset, but actual records from an Italian financial company. 5,767 employees. 309 voluntary resignations (a 5.4% attrition rate, which is realistic and makes the problem genuinely hard).

They tested four algorithms head-to-head. Random Forest crushed everything: 99.9% AUC, 94.4% sensitivity. Logistic Regression and Decision Trees weren’t even close.

They found things that don’t match your intuition. This is the best part. Using SHAP analysis, they discovered that employees who received bonuses were more likely to leave (burnout from overperforming), and employees who got retention incentives also showed higher attrition. It turns out retention incentives are targeted at people already flagged as flight risks, so the incentive is a signal of existing risk, not a cause of it.

The ML pipeline was serious work. ROSE resampling for class imbalance, 10-fold cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning, and SHAP post-processing. This took significant data science effort.

So the question became: Can RPT-1 get comparable results with a single API call?

 

Mapping the Variables to SuccessFactors

The study used 14 employee attributes. I mapped each one to the actual OData entity and field in SuccessFactors, and validated every mapping against the live API:

 

Research Variable

Where It Lives in SF

Category

Gender

PerPersonal.gender

Personal

Age Class

Derived from PerPerson.dateOfBirth

Personal

Tenure Class

Derived from EmpEmployment.startDate

People

Department

EmpJob.department

Work Environment

Rank

EmpJob.payGrade → FOPayGrade (grouped)

Management

Contract Type

EmpJob.contractType

Management

Job Level

EmpJob.payGrade

Management

Talent Program

TalentPool membership

People

Bonus Received

EmpPayCompNonRecurring existence

Management

Hours (PT/FT)

EmpJob.standardHours

Management

Retention Incentive

Custom MDF or EmpPayCompRecurring

Management

Job Family

FOJobCode.jobFunction

Management

Log Salary

EmpPayCompRecurring.paycompvalue (log10)

Management

Log Premium

Performance bonus (log10)

Management

Picture 1: Ishikawa – Fishbone diagram

 

What Is RPT-1, Exactly?

SAP RPT-1 is a Relational Pretrained Transformer , a foundation model built for tabular data, not text. Announced at TechEd 2025, it comes in three flavors:

  • sap-rpt-1-small : Fast commercial model (low latency)
  • sap-rpt-1-large : Higher accuracy commercial model
  • sap-rpt-1-oss : Open-source on GitHub (Apache 2.0)

The key idea: you give it labeled rows as examples and rows with [PREDICT] placeholders as questions. It learns from the context: no training, no feature engineering, no resampling. Think of it like few-shot prompting in ChatGPT, but for spreadsheets.

 

Running the Experiment

Step 1: Build the Dataset

I created sf_employee_attrition.csv with 50 labeled employee records using the 14 features from the research, plus 5 query rows with [PREDICT] in the ATTRITION column. The labeled data reflects the patterns the study found: higher attrition among younger, shorter-tenure employees with lower compensation, and the counterintuitive bonus/retention effects.

 

Picture 2 - sf_employee_attrition.csv, 50 labeled + 5 query rows, 14 features mapped to SF fields

 

Step 2: The No-Code Route (Playground)

Easiest way to test. Go to rpt.cloud.sap/dashboard:

  1. Upload the CSV
  2. The interface detects the [PREDICT] cells
  3. Click Predict
  4. Done

No coding. Supports up to 2,073 rows and 50 columns.

 

Step 3: The API Route (for Pipelines)

This option allows full control and evaluation metrics:

"""
SAP RPT-1 Employee Attrition Prediction
========================================
This script demonstrates how to use SAP-RPT-1 (Relational Pretrained Transformer)
to predict employee attrition using structured HR data modeled after
SuccessFactors workforce analytics dimensions.

Dataset design informed by: Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025),
"Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants",
Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.


Prerequisites:
  - For API approach: SAP RPT Playground account (https://rpt.cloud.sap)
  - For OSS approach: Python 3.11+, GPU with 24-80GB VRAM recommended

Author: Shakti Barath
"""

import json
import csv
import requests
import os
from pathlib import Path

# ============================================================
# CONFIGURATION
# ============================================================

# Option A: Load token from file (recommended)
# Create access_token.json with: {"token": "YOUR_TOKEN_HERE"}
# Get your token from: https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs (scroll to bottom)
TOKEN_FILE = "access_token.json"

# Option B: Set as environment variable
# export SAP_RPT_TOKEN="your_token_here"

RPT_API_URL = "https://rpt.cloud.sap/api/predict"
CSV_FILE = "sf_employee_attrition.csv"
TARGET_COLUMN = "ATTRITION"
PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER = "[PREDICT]"

def load_token():
    """Load API token from file or environment variable."""
    if os.path.exists(TOKEN_FILE):
        with open(TOKEN_FILE) as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            return data.get("token", "")
    return os.environ.get("SAP_RPT_TOKEN", "")

def load_csv_as_rows(filepath):
    """
    Load CSV and convert to RPT-1 row format.
    
    RPT-1 expects rows as a list of dicts. Rows with [PREDICT] in the 
    target column serve as query rows; all others are context rows
    (few-shot examples for in-context learning).
    """
    rows = []
    with open(filepath, newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        for row in reader:
            rows.append(dict(row))
    return rows

def build_payload(rows, target_column, index_column="EMP_ID"):
    """
    Build the RPT-1 API request payload.
    
    Structure:
    - rows: list of dicts (context + query rows)
    - index_column: unique identifier column
    - prediction_config: specifies target and task type
    
    Query rows contain [PREDICT] in the target column.
    Context rows contain actual labels — these are the few-shot examples.
    """
    payload = {
        "rows": rows,
        "index_column": index_column,
        "prediction_config": {
            "target_columns": [
                {
                    "name": target_column,
                    "placeholder_value": PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER,
                    "task_type": "classification"
                }
            ]
        }
    }
    return payload

def call_rpt1_api(payload, token):
    """Send prediction request to SAP RPT-1 Playground API."""
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "Accept": "application/json"
    }
    
    print("Sending prediction request to SAP RPT-1...")
    print(f"  Context rows: {sum(1 for r in payload['rows'] if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) != PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER)}")
    print(f"  Query rows:   {sum(1 for r in payload['rows'] if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) == PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER)}")
    
    response = requests.post(RPT_API_URL, headers=headers, json=payload, timeout=120)
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()

def display_predictions(result, rows):
    """Display RPT-1 predictions alongside employee context."""
    predictions = result.get("prediction", {}).get("predictions", [])
    
    # Build lookup for employee context
    emp_lookup = {str(r["EMP_ID"]): r for r in rows}
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)
    print("SAP RPT-1 ATTRITION PREDICTIONS")
    print("=" * 80)
    
    for pred in predictions:
        emp_id = str(pred.get("id", ""))
        emp = emp_lookup.get(emp_id, {})
        attrition_pred = pred.get(TARGET_COLUMN, [{}])
        
        if isinstance(attrition_pred, list) and len(attrition_pred) > 0:
            predicted_value = attrition_pred[0].get("prediction", "N/A")
            confidence = attrition_pred[0].get("confidence")
        else:
            predicted_value = "N/A"
            confidence = None
        
        risk_indicator = "HIGH RISK" if predicted_value == "Yes" else "LOW RISK"
        
        print(f"\n  Employee {emp_id}: {emp.get('JOB_FAMILY', 'Unknown')}")
        print(f"  Department: {emp.get('DEPARTMENT', '-')} | "
              f"Age: {emp.get('AGE_CLASS', '-')} | "
              f"Tenure: {emp.get('TENURE_CLASS', '-')}")
        print(f"  Talent Program: {emp.get('TALENT_PROGRAM', '-')} | "
              f"Contract: {emp.get('CONTRACT_TYPE', '-')}")
        print(f"  Prediction: {predicted_value} ({risk_indicator})")
        if confidence is not None:
            print(f"  Confidence: {confidence:.2%}")
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)

def analyze_context_patterns(rows):
    """
    Analyze attrition patterns in context data.
    Mirrors the descriptive statistics approach from the research paper
    (Manafi Varkiani et al., 2025, Table 6).
    """
    context_rows = [r for r in rows if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) != PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER]
    
    print("\n" + "-" * 60)
    print("CONTEXT DATA ANALYSIS (mirrors research methodology)")
    print("-" * 60)
    
    # Attrition by age class
    age_stats = {}
    for r in context_rows:
        age = r["AGE_CLASS"]
        left = r[TARGET_COLUMN] == "Yes"
        if age not in age_stats:
            age_stats[age] = {"total": 0, "left": 0}
        age_stats[age]["total"] += 1
        age_stats[age]["left"] += int(left)
    
    print("\nAttrition Rate by Age Class:")
    for age in sorted(age_stats.keys()):
        s = age_stats[age]
        rate = (s["left"] / s["total"] * 100) if s["total"] > 0 else 0
        print(f"  {age:12s}: {rate:5.1f}%  ({s['left']}/{s['total']})")
    
    # Attrition by tenure class
    tenure_stats = {}
    for r in context_rows:
        tenure = r["TENURE_CLASS"]
        left = r[TARGET_COLUMN] == "Yes"
        if tenure not in tenure_stats:
            tenure_stats[tenure] = {"total": 0, "left": 0}
        tenure_stats[tenure]["total"] += 1
        tenure_stats[tenure]["left"] += int(left)
    
    print("\nAttrition Rate by Tenure Class:")
    for tenure in sorted(tenure_stats.keys()):
        s = tenure_stats[tenure]
        rate = (s["left"] / s["total"] * 100) if s["total"] > 0 else 0
        print(f"  {tenure:12s}: {rate:5.1f}%  ({s['left']}/{s['total']})")
    
    # Key insight from the research
    print("\n  Key Insight (Manafi Varkiani et al., 2025):")
    print("  Younger employees and those with shorter tenure show")
    print("  significantly higher attrition rates. The research found")
    print("  15.9% attrition for age 18-25 vs 1.4% for age 50-65.")

# ============================================================
# MAIN EXECUTION
# ============================================================

def main():
    print("=" * 80)
    print("SAP RPT-1: Employee Attrition Prediction Experiment")
    print("Grounded in: Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025), ESWA 272")
    print("=" * 80)
    
    # Load data
    if not Path(CSV_FILE).exists():
        print(f"ERROR: {CSV_FILE} not found. Place it in the same directory.")
        return
    
    rows = load_csv_as_rows(CSV_FILE)
    print(f"\nLoaded {len(rows)} employee records from {CSV_FILE}")
    
    # Analyze context patterns
    analyze_context_patterns(rows)
    
    # Load token
    token = load_token()
    if not token:
        print("\n" + "-" * 60)
        print("NO API TOKEN FOUND")
        print("-" * 60)
        print("To run predictions with the RPT Playground API:")
        print("1. Sign up at https://rpt.cloud.sap")
        print("2. Copy your token from https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs")
        print("3. Save it to access_token.json:")
        print('   {"token": "YOUR_TOKEN_HERE"}')
        print("\nAlternatively, use the OSS version (see rpt1_oss_predict.py)")
        return
    
    # Build payload and predict
    payload = build_payload(rows, TARGET_COLUMN)
    
    try:
        result = call_rpt1_api(payload, token)
        display_predictions(result, rows)
        
        # Save raw response
        with open("rpt1_predictions.json", "w") as f:
            json.dump(result, f, indent=2)
        print(f"\nRaw predictions saved to rpt1_predictions.json")
        
    except requests.exceptions.HTTPError as e:
        print(f"\nAPI Error: {e}")
        print("Check your token and ensure the playground is accessible.")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"\nError: {e}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Create access_token.json in the same folder with your RPT-1 API key:

{
  "token": "YOUR_RPT-API_KEY"
}

 

The Results

I ran the CSV through the RPT Playground API. Here’s exactly what came back:

EMP ID

Profile

Prediction

Confidence

Why It Makes Sense

1051

Finance • Staff • Permanent

Yes

95%

Age 25–30, Tenure 0–3 yr, Talent Program, Low premium

1052

IT • Staff • Permanent

No

98%

Age 30–40, Tenure 10–20 yr, Bonus received, Strong salary

1053

Sales • Staff • Fixed-Term

Yes

99%

Age 18–25, Tenure 0–3 yr, Fixed-term, Lowest salary

1054

HR • Manager • Permanent

No

99%

Age 40–50, Tenure 10–20 yr, Manager rank, High comp

1055

Ops • Staff • Permanent

Yes

94%

Age 25–30, Talent Prog, Retention incentive, Lower salary

 

Accuracy

Avg Confidence

Inference Time

Context Rows

5/5 (100%)

97%

399 ms

50 labeled rows

 

What Stood Out

The easy calls were easy. Employee 1053 (young, short-tenure, fixed-term, lowest salary) and Employee 1054 (senior manager, long tenure, high pay) both got 99% confidence. These are the extremes, exactly where the research paper’s Random Forest also showed the clearest separation.

Employee 1055 was the interesting one. This person has moderate tenure (3–10 years), which in the training data strongly correlates with staying. But RPT-1 correctly flagged them as at-risk at 94% confidence. Why? The combination of a retention incentive, talent program membership, and lower compensation. Remember what the research found: retention incentives are a signal that someone is already a flight risk, not a shield against it. RPT-1 picked up that interaction from just 50 context rows. That’s impressive.

399 milliseconds. The research paper required ROSE resampling, 10-fold cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning across four algorithms, and SHAP post-processing. RPT-1 got directionally equivalent predictions with a single API call on raw CSV data. For an HR team that wants a quick risk screening before committing to a full ML build, that’s a big deal.

The honest caveat: This is a 50-row synthetic dataset with intentionally clear patterns. Production data with 5,767 employees and a 5.4% attrition rate would be a harder test. These results show RPT-1 can read tabular patterns well. A head-to-head on real production data is the next step.

 

What RPT-1 Is Good At Here

  • No ML pipeline required. The research team spent serious effort on resampling, cross-validation, and algorithm selection. RPT-1 skips all of that.
  • Handles mixed data out of the box. Categorical and numerical fields go in as-is. No encoding needed.
  • Tolerates missing data. The research had to drop 5 variables due to gaps. RPT-1 handles missing values by design.
  • Easy to iterate. Want to add engagement survey scores? Just add a column. No pipeline rebuild.

 

What RPT-1 Can’t Do (Yet)

  • No explainability. RPT-1 gives you a prediction and a confidence score, but not why. For HR decisions, that matters a lot.
  • Class imbalance is your problem. With a 5.4% attrition rate, your context rows need careful curation.
  • No time-series awareness. RPT-1 works on static snapshots. It can’t predict when someone will leave.
  • Playground has limits. 2,073 rows max. For production scale, deploy via SAP AI Core.

 

So What Should You Actually Do With This?

RPT-1 makes it trivially easy to generate attrition predictions from SuccessFactors data. But predictions without understanding can lead to bad interventions. (Remember the retention incentive example: a naïve reading suggests incentives cause attrition.)

My recommendation:

  1. Use RPT-1 as a rapid screening tool. Upload your SF extract, get a risk score, identify who needs a closer look.
  2. Build a traditional ML model in parallel for explainability. Run the same features through Random Forest with SHAP to understand the why.
  3. Loop in HR. Especially for counterintuitive patterns. Data literacy plus domain knowledge turns a prediction into an action.
  4. Don’t treat aggregate results as universal. Attrition drivers for one job family can differ completely from the company-wide pattern.

 

References

  • Manafi Varkiani, S., Pattarin, F., Fabbri, T., & Fantoni, G. (2025). Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants. Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.
  • Lundberg, S. M., & Lee, S. I. (2017). A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions. NeurIPS 2017.
  • SAP-RPT-1: sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/sap-rpt.html
  • SAP-RPT-1-OSS: github.com/SAP-samples/sap-rpt-1-oss
  • RPT Playground: rpt.cloud.sap
  • Reference Blog — Carrier Prediction in SAP TM: SAP Community (Mar 2026)

Happy experimenting!

Built with synthetic data mirroring the variable structure and patterns from Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025). No real employee data was used. Results are illustrative.
